frameworks.name: spatial Bayesian GLM
frameworks.one_line: cortical-surface GLM leveraging spatial dependencies among neighboring vertices

frameworks.name: classical massive-univariate GLM
frameworks.one_line: vertex-wise GLM ignoring neighborhood similarity in activation patterns

frameworks.name: cortical surface-based fMRI analysis
frameworks.one_line: analysis on subject-specific surface meshes rather than volumetric voxels

frameworks.name: multi-run hierarchical extension
frameworks.one_line: extends spatial Bayesian GLM to combine multiple runs per subject

core_question: Does a cortical surface-based spatial Bayesian GLM yield more reliable and powerful task-activation estimates than the classical GLM in individuals and groups?
key_assumptions: neighboring cortical vertices share similar activation patterns
spatial priors improve estimates of task-evoked responses
subject-specific cortical surfaces are appropriate analysis units
manipulations: modeling approach (spatial Bayesian GLM vs. classical GLM)
sample size used for group inference
key_findings: spatial Bayesian GLM yields highly reliable activations in individual subjects
method is powerful enough to detect trait-like functional topologies
power to detect scientifically meaningful effects is nearly invariant to sample size
interpretations: leveraging spatial dependencies produces more accurate and powerful activation maps than massive-univariate GLM
surface-based spatial Bayesian GLM is suitable for individual-level functional mapping
